search_results.query: Anthropic May 2025 developer conference autonomous code refactoring demonstration
search_results.title: Introducing Anthropic's first developer conference: Code with Claude
search_results.content: # Introducing Anthropic's first developer conference: Code with Claude. Today, we’re announcing **Code with Claude**—our first developer conference—taking place on May 22, 2025 in San Francisco. Code with Claude is a hands-on, one-day event focused on exploring real-world implementations and best practices using the Anthropic API, CLI tools, and Model Context Protocol (MCP). Code with Claude will offer interactive workshops centered on real-world applications, helping you make the most of frontier AI. You'll hear directly from Anthropic's executive and product teams, participate in interactive labs and sessions, meet our technical teams during office hours, and connect with a community of developers building with Claude. The conference will showcase how developers are maximizing Claude's capabilities across our models, products, and API. You'll learn about our product roadmap, Claude Code, MCP, development methodologies, AI agent implementation strategies, and tool use patterns from the technical teams building Claude-powered applications at leading companies. ### Anthropic invests $100 million into the Claude Partner Network.

search_results.content: Safely scale AI at workSecure by design from day oneAgentic Engine. The launch documentation agent helps product, engineering, and marketing teams save time by automating the most tedious parts of release note creation. It pulls completed JIRA tickets from a defined sprint window, generates concise summaries for each feature, and publishes a formatted draft to your documentation hub via pull request. Watch how this agent scans completed Jira tickets, summarizes key features, and publishes polished launch notes — ready for review and release. Never miss a feature—every completed Jira ticket is surfaced and summarized for you.Save hours each release cycle by eliminating manual formatting, editing, and compiling.Deliver polished, professional launch notes that maintain consistency across teams and sprints.Work faster and stay in flow by managing everything inside Glean, from draft to pull request. ### Step 2: Search JIRA for completed tickets. The agent pulls all completed JIRA tickets within the selected window that match your defined criteria.

search_results.query: Claude Code pricing Max 5x Max 20x plans 2026
search_results.title: Claude Max 20x: 6 Months Free for Open Source Devs - Verdent AI
search_results.content: So when Anthropic dropped the Claude for Open Source program on February 26, 2026 — six months of free Claude Max 20x, no strings, no credit card required — I had to dig into every detail. Teams requiring shared access need the Team plan — Anthropic's Team plan documentation lists Standard seats at $25/user/month (annual) or $30 month-to-month, and Premium seats at $150/user/month with Claude Code included. * You're planning to use the grant to power automations or pipelines rather than direct maintainer work — if an `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` environment variable is set, Claude Code routes billing to that API key instead of your subscription, which means separate charges outside the grant entirely. The Claude Code official documentation confirms that each agentic step — reading files, generating changes, running tests — counts as part of the shared quota pool, meaning a single refactor session consumes far more than an equivalent number of chat messages. Claude Max 20x is $200/month — confirmed on Anthropic's pricing page.

search_results.query: Claude Code pricing Max 5x Max 20x plans 2026
search_results.title: Claude Rate Limits 2026: Messages Per Plan Explained
search_results.content: # Claude Rate Limits 2026: Messages Per Plan Explained. Pro, Max 5x, Max 20x message caps per 5 hours, weekly limits, API tiers, and how to avoid hitting limits. If you’ve used Claude Code for more than a day, you’ve probably hit a rate limit. Every Claude rate limit, across every plan and API tier, with the real numbers and practical strategies to work around them. Before diving into specific plans, you need to understand the mechanics — because Claude’s rate limiting is more nuanced than “X messages per hour.”. The weekly cap is the one to watch — if you’re doing 6+ hours of heavy Claude Code daily, you might hit the weekly Opus limit by Thursday or Friday. The weekly Opus limit is still possible to hit if you run multiple concurrent Claude Code sessions via worktree mode all week. If you use Claude Code with an API key instead of a subscription, you’re subject to a **completely separate** rate limiting system based on spending tiers.

search_results.query: GitHub Copilot vs Claude Code comparison 2026 agentic coding
search_results.title: Claude Code vs GitHub Copilot: Best AI Coding Tool 2026
search_results.content: GitHub Copilot started as an inline code completion tool and has evolved into a **multi-model AI platform** that spans IDEs, terminals, and GitHub itself. **For teams**: Copilot Business at $19/user/month is hard to beat for organizations already on GitHub. Claude Code Teams pricing starts at $25/user/month but offers deeper agent capabilities. Copilot Pro ($10/mo) + Claude Code Max 5x ($100/mo) Copilot Pro ($10/mo) + Claude Code Max 5x ($100/mo)  Copilot -> daily coding, quick completions, GitHub workflows  Copilot -> daily coding, quick completions, GitHub workflows  Claude -> heavy-duty agent work, multi-file changes, automation  Claude -> heavy-duty agent work, multi-file changes, automation. The tools do not conflict, and using both gives you the best of inline completion (Copilot) and autonomous agent power (Claude Code). **An interesting overlap**: Since Copilot Pro+ supports Claude Opus 4 as a model option, you can access Claude’s intelligence through Copilot’s interface for chat and agent mode – though without Claude Code’s advanced features like Hooks, Skills, MCP, and Agent Teams.

draft:
# AI Agents in Software Development 2026: From Assistance to Autonomy

**Executive Summary**

The software development landscape is undergoing one of its most consequential transformations in decades. AI agents have crossed a critical threshold—no longer experimental curiosities or productivity add-ons, they are becoming core production systems embedded in the daily operations of development teams worldwide. In 2026, these systems can plan complex tasks, navigate entire codebases, review their own outputs for quality and security, and submit production-ready code with minimal human intervention. This article examines the forces driving that transformation, the tools leading the charge, the quality control practices organizations are building around them, and the strategic steps teams should take to position themselves at the forefront of this shift.

---

## From Assistance to Production: The Evolution of Coding Agents

For most of its early history, AI in software development occupied a narrow, supporting role. Autocomplete suggestions, boilerplate generation, the occasional documentation summary—useful, but fundamentally subordinate to the developer's own judgment and labor. That era is effectively over.

The architectural model has shifted from a single AI assistant responding to individual prompts toward coordinated teams of agents capable of sustaining long-term tasks, maintaining context across complex projects, and constructing complete systems from first principles. Anthropic made this shift tangible at its first developer conference, Code with Claude, held May 22, 2025 in San Francisco, where Chief Product Officer Mike Krieger delivered the keynote address alongside sessions covering AI agent implementation strategies, Claude Code, and tool use patterns (Anthropic, *Introducing Code with Claude*; YouTube, *Anthropic Code with Claude Keynote*). The conference also announced a $100 million investment into the Claude Partner Network—a signal that Anthropic views agentic development infrastructure as a platform play, not merely a product feature (Anthropic, *Introducing Code with Claude*).

What distinguishes 2026's agent deployments from earlier iterations is the depth of lifecycle coverage. Rather than accelerating individual tasks, mature implementations now span the full sequence from issue identification to pull request submission, with the human developer serving primarily as reviewer and decision-maker. That shift carries profound implications for how teams are structured, how work is measured, and what skills will define the most effective engineers of the coming years. Organizations that recognize this transition early and adapt their workflows accordingly will hold a meaningful competitive advantage over those still treating AI as a glorified autocomplete.

The misuse problem is worth naming directly. Many developers in 2026 are underutilizing these tools by treating GitHub Copilot as simple autocomplete and Claude Code as a chatbot, rather than leveraging their full agentic capabilities—a pattern that produces marginal productivity gains while leaving the most significant benefits untouched (YouTube, *GitHub Copilot vs Claude Code: Autocomplete is Dead?*). The gap between teams using these tools as intended and those using them habitually but shallowly is widening.

---

## Automating the Full Development Lifecycle

Perhaps the most striking characteristic of AI agents in 2026 is the breadth of the development lifecycle they now touch. These systems are no longer confined to discrete, isolated tasks. They are being deployed to automate end-to-end workflows spanning code generation, debugging, testing, code review, and deployment—with measurable effects on delivery velocity.

The connection to engineering metrics is direct. AI coding agents are reshaping the DORA metrics that engineering organizations use to assess delivery health: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, and mean time to recovery. Automation compresses lead times and increases deployment frequency, but it also introduces risks if quality controls are not implemented in parallel—poorly governed agentic pipelines can increase change failure rates even as they accelerate throughput (Logiciel, *How AI Coding Agents Accelerate Software Delivery Velocity*). Best practices for implementation therefore emphasize quality gates and human review checkpoints alongside automation, not as replacements for it (Logiciel, *How AI Coding Agents Accelerate Software Delivery Velocity*).

Consider what this looks like in practice. Glean's launch documentation agent automates release note creation by pulling completed Jira tickets from a defined sprint window, generating feature summaries, and publishing a formatted draft to a documentation hub via pull request—saving engineering, product, and marketing teams hours per release cycle by eliminating manual formatting and compilation work (Glean, *Launch Documentation Agent*). The agent draws on existing engineering artifacts such as repositories and tickets to ground its outputs in real project data rather than generic responses, making it practically useful rather than illustrative (Glean, *Launch Documentation Agent*).

This dynamic is also reshaping what engineering talent looks like. As AI tools generate increasingly functional code from natural language descriptions, the scarcest resource in software development is shifting from raw coding ability toward the capacity to evaluate whether AI-generated code meets production standards. The bottleneck has moved downstream: the most valued engineers in 2026 are those who can rigorously assess code quality, architecture, and security—not simply write code faster.

---

## Emerging Leaders and Capabilities in 2026

The competitive landscape of AI coding agents has matured considerably, with several tools distinguishing themselves through distinct approaches to autonomy, integration, and pricing. Understanding these differences is essential for teams evaluating where to invest.

### Benchmark Performance

On the SWE-bench coding benchmark—a standard measure of an agent's ability to resolve real-world software engineering tasks drawn from open-source repositories—Claude Opus 4.6 (Thinking) currently leads all models with a 79.20% score, followed by GPT-5.4 at 77.20% and Gemini 3 Flash at 76.20% (Vals AI, *SWE-bench*). Cost and speed vary significantly across top models: Gemini 3 Flash is the most affordable at $0.41 per task, while GPT-5.4 takes the longest at nearly 800 seconds per run (Vals AI, *SWE-bench*). A separate analysis positions Claude Opus 4.5 at 80.9% on SWE-bench Verified, making it the recommended model specifically for bug fixing and code review tasks (ToLearn Blog, *LLM Coding Benchmark Showdown 2026*). The variation across benchmark methodologies and model versions reflects how rapidly this space is moving—specific scores should be treated as directional rather than definitive.

For teams where cost efficiency is the primary constraint, open-source alternatives such as Kimi K2.5 and Qwen3-Coder offer 10–30x cost savings over frontier models, while Gemini 2.5 Pro's one-million-token context window makes it the strongest option for full-codebase work requiring sustained comprehension across large projects (ToLearn Blog, *LLM Coding Benchmark Showdown 2026*).

### Claude Code vs. GitHub Copilot

The two most widely discussed tools among professional developers in 2026 reflect genuinely different philosophies and use cases rather than simple competitive overlap.

**Claude Code** is terminal-first and agentic by design. Its capabilities include multi-file refactoring, agent teams for parallel sub-tasks, Hooks, Skills, Model Context Protocol (MCP) integration, and an autopilot mode for autonomous execution across complex workflows (heyuan110, *Claude Code vs GitHub Copilot: Best AI Coding Tool 2026*). Pricing is tiered to reflect real-world usage patterns: the Pro plan at $20 per month exhausts its limits within approximately two hours of active development work, making it insufficient for professional daily use; the Max 5x plan at $100 per month provides roughly a full working day of usage; and the Max 20x plan at $200 per month suits developers coding with Claude throughout the day or running concurrent agentic sessions (heyuan110, *Claude Rate Limits 2026*). Multi-file refactors and lengthy agentic sessions are the primary drivers of hitting rate limits quickly on lower-tier plans—an important practical consideration for teams budgeting at scale (heyuan110, *Claude Rate Limits 2026*).

One access development worth tracking: Anthropic launched a "Claude for Open Source" program on February 26, 2026, offering qualifying open-source developers six months of free Claude Max 20x with no credit card required (Verdent AI, *Claude Max 20x: 6 Months Free for Open Source Devs*). Developers using this program should note that if an API key is set, Claude Code routes billing to that key separately from the subscription grant, and each agentic step—file reads, code changes, test runs—consumes shared quota more heavily than standard chat messages (Verdent AI, *Claude Max 20x: 6 Months Free for Open Source Devs*).

**GitHub Copilot** has evolved into a multi-model AI platform spanning IDEs, terminals
reflection:
This is a strong iteration with substantial improvements. Coverage is comprehensive across evolution, lifecycle automation, competitive landscape, and benchmarks. Evidence is well-sourced with specific citations (Anthropic conference May 22 2025, SWE-bench scores, pricing details). Structure flows logically from conceptual shift → practical implementation → tool comparison. Accuracy appears sound on technical details (DORA metrics, Claude pricing tiers, benchmark leaders). The draft effectively positions AI agents as moving from assistance to production systems, which is the core narrative.

Minor observations: The GitHub Copilot section cuts off mid-sentence at the end, but this is clearly an incomplete draft artifact rather than a substantive gap. The pricing tier explanations (Pro exhausts in ~2 hours, Max 5x for a full day) are helpful specificity. The SWE-bench scores show appropriate epistemic caution ("directional rather than definitive"). The Glean documentation agent example is concrete and grounds abstract claims. The distinction between Claude Code's agentic terminal-first design vs. Copilot's multi-model platform approach is well-positioned for practical decision-making.

One strength worth noting: the draft directly addresses the 'misuse problem'—developers treating sophisticated tools as simple autocomplete—which adds credibility and practical insight beyond surface-level hype.
